scenario: Entry: TikTok strategy for musicians — what actually works in 2026

input.user_message: How should I be using TikTok as a musician? I feel like everyone says to post there but nothing I try works.

expected.output_sample:
Most musicians fail on TikTok because they treat it like Instagram — polished, promotional, 'check out my new song.' TikTok rewards the opposite: raw, personal, pattern-interrupting content. Here's what actually works in 2026:

**The 80/20 rule:** 80% of your TikToks should NOT be 'listen to my song.' They should be: stories from your life as an artist, hot takes about your genre, behind-the-scenes of making music, reaction/duet content, and personality-driven clips that happen to have your music in the background. 20% can be direct promotion.

**The 3-second hook:** TikTok's algorithm decides in the first 3 seconds whether to show your video to more people. Every video needs an instant hook: a provocative statement, a visual surprise, or a text overlay that creates curiosity ('I spent $5,000 making a song that got 200 streams. Here's what I learned.').

**What to do with YOUR music on TikTok:**
- Identify a 15-second 'hookable' snippet from your song (usually the chorus or a catchy pre-chorus moment)
- Create 5-10 DIFFERENT videos using that same snippet as the sound — different concepts, angles, stories
- If one format takes off, make 10 more variations of THAT specific format
- Post 1-3 times per day for 30 days minimum before evaluating what's working

**What NOT to do:**
- Don't post a video of your song playing with your cover art as the visual. That's an ad, not content.
- Don't buy followers or engagement — the algorithm will shadowban you
- Don't use trending sounds from OTHER artists when you're trying to promote YOUR music

**The uncomfortable truth:** TikTok virality is unpredictable. The business value isn't going viral once — it's building a consistent audience that shows up for your releases. 5,000 engaged followers who actually stream your music is worth more than 100,000 followers who watched one funny video and never thought about you again.

scenario: Expert: Email list as business infrastructure — the asset you own

input.user_message: Everyone says I need an email list but I have 50k Instagram followers. Why would I need email when I can reach my fans on social?

expected.output_sample:
Because you don't own your Instagram followers — Meta does. And they can change the rules anytime.

**The harsh numbers:**
- Instagram organic reach in 2026: 3-7% of your followers see any given post. Of your 50k followers, maybe 2,500-3,500 actually see your release day announcement.
- Email open rate for music: 25-40%. If you had 5,000 email subscribers, 1,250-2,000 would see your message — and they actively chose to open it.
- Email click-through rate: 2-5%. Instagram link-click rate from posts: effectively 0% (you can't even put a link in a post).

**Why email is a business asset and social followers aren't:**
1. **You own it.** If Instagram disappears or bans you or changes the algorithm, your email list goes with you. Vine died. Musical.ly became TikTok. Platforms change. Your email list is permanent.
2. **Higher conversion.** Email converts to sales, streams, and ticket purchases at 5-10x the rate of social posts. When you email 'new album out today' with a direct link, people click it.
3. **You control delivery.** No algorithm deciding if your message gets shown. You hit send, it arrives.
4. **Segmentation.** You can email fans in specific cities about tours, email superfans about exclusive merch drops, email casual listeners about new releases. Instagram is one-to-all.

**How to build it from your existing audience:**
- Link-in-bio landing page with email signup (offer an exclusive track, early access, or behind-the-scenes content as an incentive)
- At live shows: 'text this number to join' SMS-to-email capture
- Every pre-save link should include an email capture option
- Target: convert 2-5% of your social followers to email. 50k Instagram → 1,000-2,500 email subscribers is a realistic starting goal.

**Tool recommendation:** Mailchimp (free up to 500 contacts), ConvertKit ($29/month, better for creators), or Beehiiv (free tier available). Start today.

scenario: Entry: Content calendar for musicians — what to post and when

input.user_message: I never know what to post on social media. Can you give me a weekly content schedule?

expected.output_sample:
Consistency beats inspiration. Here's a repeatable weekly framework you can maintain even on busy weeks:

**Monday — Studio/Process:**
Post a behind-the-scenes clip from your creative process: a beat being made, a vocal take, lyrics being written, a mix in progress. This is your most 'authentic' content day. Format: 15-30 second Reel or TikTok.

**Tuesday — Engage:**
No post. Instead, spend 20 minutes engaging with other artists and fans in your genre: comment on their posts, respond to stories, duet/stitch a relevant TikTok. The algorithm rewards your content when you're active on the platform. This is free marketing.

**Wednesday — Personality/Story:**
Share something about YOU — not your music. A personal story, a hot take about your genre, something funny that happened, a day-in-the-life moment. This builds the parasocial relationship that turns casual listeners into fans.

**Thursday — Music Feature:**
Promote your music directly: a snippet of your latest release, a lyric card, a streaming link, or a 'have you heard this one?' reintroduction of an older track. Only 1-2 days/week should be direct promotion. This is one of them.

**Friday — Release or Throwback:**
If you're releasing music, this is the day. If not, post a throwback (old photo, early recording, 'this time last year') or share a playlist you made featuring your music alongside artists you admire.

**Saturday/Sunday — Stories only:**
Keep the pressure low on weekends. Post to stories: what you're listening to, what you're doing, quick polls. The bar is lower for stories — they can be raw, unplanned, and ephemeral.

**The non-negotiable:** Post at LEAST 3 times per week on your main feed and 5+ stories per week. If you can only manage one thing, it should be the studio/process content on Monday — that consistently performs best for musicians across every platform.

**Batch it:** Film 5-7 clips on one day, edit them, and schedule them out for the week (Meta Business Suite for Instagram, TikTok's built-in scheduler). 2 hours of batch filming = a week of content.

scenario: How to grow on social media as an independent artist

input.user_message: I've been posting consistently but my follower count isn't growing. What am I doing wrong?

expected.output_sample:
Consistent posting without growth usually means one of these:

1. No hook in the first 3 seconds: Algorithms serve content based on watch time and completion rate. If your videos don't grab attention immediately, people scroll, the algorithm sees low completion, and stops showing it. Every video needs an opening moment that creates a reason to keep watching.

2. No discoverable context: Are you posting into a niche? The algorithm recommends to people who like what they know. 'Musician posts music' is too broad. 'Producer explains how to make a Detroit boom bap beat in 60 seconds' has a built-in audience.

3. Posting for followers instead of value: Content that teaches, shows a process, or makes someone feel something spreads. Content that just says 'listen to my music' performs like an ad — people ignore ads.

4. Platform mismatch: Are you using the right platform for your genre and style? TikTok rewards personality and short-form hooks. Instagram rewards aesthetics. YouTube rewards depth and searchability. Posting the same content everywhere equally is rarely optimal.

Which platform is this about and what does your typical post look like?

notes: Algorithm growth education — concrete diagnostics rather than vague 'post more' advice.

scenario: Expert: YouTube algorithm optimization — end screens, cards, and the first 48-hour window

input.user_message: I uploaded my music video 2 days ago and it got decent early views but now it's dropped off completely. What drives YouTube's algorithm in the first 48 hours and how do I optimize for it going forward?

**The 4 metrics YouTube evaluates in the first 48 hours:**
1. **CTR (Click-Through Rate):** What percentage of people shown your thumbnail actually click. Benchmark: 4-10% is good for music videos. Low CTR = YouTube stops showing it. Fix: test different thumbnails within the first 24 hours using YouTube Studio's A/B thumbnail feature (requires >1,000 subscribers).
2. **Average View Duration / Audience Retention:** What percentage of the video people watch. For a 3-minute music video: >60% average is good; >70% signals viral potential. If your video has a slow intro, this metric kills distribution.
3. **Velocity of engagement:** Views, likes, comments, shares in the first 24 hours relative to your channel's baseline. Releasing to your email list, Discord, and stories simultaneously in the first 2 hours creates this velocity.
4. **Session time:** Does watching your video lead viewers to watch more YouTube? Videos that keep viewers on the platform get amplified. End screens to your other videos increase this.

**What to do NOW (still within the first week):**
- Edit your thumbnail if CTR is below 3% (YouTube Studio → Content → select video → Custom thumbnail)
- Add end screens pointing to your 3 best-performing videos (20-second window at video end)
- Add a card linking to your channel at the 30-second mark
- Create a YouTube Short (under 60 seconds) using the best visual moment from the video, linking to the full video in the description — Shorts feed main video traffic

**Long-term signals that tell YouTube to keep promoting:**
- Subscribers gained from the video (tracked per-video)
- Comments with replies (conversation signals community)
- Shares outside YouTube (WhatsApp, text — increases external impression value)

**The algorithm does NOT care about:** Upload time of day, number of hashtags, or subscriber count (for surfacing to non-subscribers).

scenario: Expert: Diagnosing and recovering from a TikTok 'shadowban' or algorithm penalty

input.user_message: My TikTok views dropped from 50K-100K per video to under 2K literally overnight 3 weeks ago. Nothing was removed, no notifications, no strikes. My hashtags aren't appearing in For You page searches anymore. I've heard this is called a 'shadowban.' What actually happened algorithmically, how do I confirm it, and what's the recovery process?

expected.output_sample:
What you're describing is consistent with an algorithm distribution penalty — TikTok doesn't use the term 'shadowban' officially, but it's a real reduction in distribution that affects some accounts. Here's the diagnostic and recovery process.

**Step 1 — Confirm It's Actually an Algorithm Penalty (vs. Normal Variance):**
Run the TikTok Shadowban Test:
1. Post a video with a specific, unusual hashtag (something like #[YourNameSBTest2025]) that nobody else would use
2. Log out of TikTok completely
3. Search that hashtag on TikTok
4. If your video appears: you're NOT shadowbanned, your view drop is likely content or algorithm change related
5. If your video does NOT appear (even though you just posted with it): your account has reduced hashtag discovery reach

Also check: ask 2-3 friends to search your username directly. If they can find your account normally, it's algorithm distribution restriction, not account restriction.

**What Actually Happens Algorithmically:**
TikTok's algorithm restricts distribution when it detects patterns associated with:
- **Spam posting behavior**: posting 5+ times per day, or using mass automation
- **Unusual engagement patterns**: buying fake followers/views (bot engagement is detectable)
- **Content policy strikes** (even if a video wasn't removed — some borderline content gets 'limited' not removed)
- **Copyright issues**: if you used music that triggered a Content ID claim
- **Device or IP flags**: using scheduling tools that TikTok's API flags as non-native, or VPN usage

**Step 2 — Audit the 3 Weeks Before the Drop:**
When exactly did the drop start? What changed in the 5 days before?
- Did you post more frequently than usual?
- Did you use a piece of music that could have triggered a copyright flag?
- Did you start using a third-party scheduling tool?
- Did you get any comment or DM content removed?
- Did a video perform extremely well (viral) which sometimes triggers a temporary 'cooldown'?

**Step 3 — Recovery Protocol (21 Days):**

**Days 1-7: Stop and Reset**
- Post nothing for 48-72 hours. This 'breaks' any negative signal cycle.
- Remove any third-party scheduling tools — post only from TikTok's native app
- Remove any third-party login sessions from TikTok's security settings

**Days 7-14: Controlled Re-entry**
- Post 1 video every 48 hours (not daily — signal quality over quantity)
- Use ONLY original sounds or properly licensed music
- Engage authentically: respond to every comment on your new videos, spend 20 minutes/day commenting on other creators' content in your niche
- Use trending formats and sounds that are actively growing in your niche
- Keep videos to 15-30 seconds for highest completion rate (completion rate is the primary FYP ranking signal)

**Days 14-21: Gradual Resume**
- If Week 2 shows improvement (even modest — 3K-10K views), resume normal cadence
- Post a high-effort, high-quality video on Day 21 as a 'reset' anchor

**Timeline Expectation:**
Most accounts see measurable improvement within 2-4 weeks of the recovery protocol. Some accounts take 6-8 weeks. If there's been no improvement after 8 weeks of clean posting, consider creating a new account and migrating your audience gradually.

notes: Expert TikTok algorithm penalty — diagnostic confirmation test, root cause taxonomy, 21-day recovery protocol, timeline expectations.

scenario: Expert: Rebuilding TikTok growth after an algorithm reset — posting strategy and engagement signals

input.user_message: I've done the 48-hour pause. My views are slowly returning but I'm still at 3K-8K per video when I used to get 30K-80K. I've been posting for 2 weeks consistently. What's the specific posting strategy to accelerate recovery — frequency, timing, format, engagement signals I should be optimizing for?

3K-8K views after a 2-week consistent posting effort is a positive signal — the penalty is lifting. Here's how to accelerate the recovery.

**The 3 Engagement Signals That Drive FYP Algorithm Recovery:**

**Signal 1 — Completion Rate (Most Important)**
TikTok's algorithm weighs completion rate above all other metrics in the recovery phase. A video where 60%+ of viewers watch to the end is the strongest signal the algorithm can receive from your account.
How to maximize: Keep videos under 20 seconds for the next 2 weeks. Hook must land in the first 2 seconds (the 'stop-scroll' moment). No slow intros, no dead air. Cut ruthlessly.

**Signal 2 — Comments per View**
Comments are a high-engagement signal. Target: 1 comment per 100 views (1% comment rate = healthy). How to drive comments: End videos with an unresolved question or a mildly controversial statement. 'I could never date someone who doesn't like [X]' generates more comments than 'Here's my new song.' Reply to EVERY comment in the first 2 hours — this extends the video's display time in the algorithm.

**Signal 3 — Profile Visits from Video**
When someone clicks through from a video to your profile, the algorithm interprets strong interest in YOU specifically (not just the content). In your video bio section, use a curiosity gap: 'See the full story on my profile.' This drives profile click-through rate.

**Posting Frequency and Timing:**
Recovery phase: 1 video/day (not more). Two videos per day during recovery is counterproductive — each video competes with your other video for the algorithm's distribution budget.
Best posting windows for music content (general — verify with your TikTok analytics):
- 7-9am your audience's timezone (morning scroll)
- 12-2pm (lunch break)
- 7-10pm (evening peak)
Post once, at one of these windows. Track which window gets best completion rate, then use that exclusively.

**Format Strategy for Recovery:**
For the next 3 weeks, use ONLY these formats (all proven high-completion-rate formats for music accounts):
1. **'Behind the beat'**: 15-second clip of you making a sound or moment in the production process that feels satisfying/surprising
2. **Duet with a trending audio**: Duets keep the trending audio's momentum and expose your account to that trend's existing audience
3. **Comment response video**: Reply to a comment on your earlier videos with a new video — this resurfaces your older content AND creates new content simultaneously

scenario: Expert: Protecting a healthy TikTok account from algorithm penalties — long-term risk management

Welcome back to 40-80K views. Now let's make sure the next algorithm shift doesn't cost you 6 weeks. Protection has two parts: hygiene practices that prevent penalties, and platform redundancy that survives them.

**Part 1 — Account Hygiene Practices:**

**1. Native posting only:**
Never use third-party scheduling tools that post automatically to TikTok on your behalf. TikTok's API detects non-native posting patterns and flags them. If you need scheduling: use TikTok's native scheduler (available in Creator Studio) — it's built-in and algorithm-safe.

**2. Engagement authenticity:**
Never buy views, followers, or likes. TikTok's bot detection has become sophisticated — it identifies fake engagement and penalizes the receiving account, not just the seller. Even 'high quality' view purchases get detected over time as engagement patterns don't match real accounts.

**3. Content Policy proactive audit:**
Before posting any video, run a quick mental checklist:
- Does the music have clear rights? (If in doubt, use TikTok's music library — zero Content ID risk)
- Is any text, audio, or imagery potentially flagged? (Nudity, violence, political content even in the background)
- Is any person identifiable in the background who hasn't consented?

**4. Posting frequency ceiling:**
Maximum 2 videos per day consistently. 3+ videos/day over extended periods flags spam-like behavior. Best sustainable cadence: 1 video/day, with 1 high-effort video 3× per week.

**5. Regularly update your TikTok app:**
Outdated app versions can trigger algorithm flags due to using deprecated API parameters. Auto-update enabled on your device.

**Part 2 — Platform Redundancy (So You Never Start from Zero):**

**Build parallel owned channels now — while your TikTok is healthy:**
| Channel | Target | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Email list | 2,000+ subscribers | Not affected by any algorithm change |
| Instagram (Reels) | 50% of TikTok following | Content reuse; different algorithm |
| YouTube (Shorts + long-form) | Growing | Indexed forever; different discovery |
| Text/SMS (Community.com) | 500+ subscribers | 95% open rate; algorithm-proof |

**The cross-posting habit:**
Every TikTok you post should also go to Instagram Reels and YouTube Shorts the same day. This takes 5 minutes and ensures each piece of content builds equity on three platforms simultaneously. When TikTok's algorithm shifts, your Reels and Shorts audiences continue growing independently.

**The 30-day audience migration habit:**
Once per month, use a TikTok video to actively drive your audience to your email list: 'If TikTok goes down tomorrow, find me here: [link].' You've already built the fanbase — protect it by migrating them to owned channels before you need to.

notes: Expert TikTok account protection — native posting, bot detection awareness, content audit checklist, platform redundancy strategy, monthly audience migration habit.

scenario: Expert: Managing a community crisis when toxic behavior emerges

input.user_message: My comment section on a controversial post is being overrun with toxic replies and harassment of other fans. It's becoming a hostile environment that's driving away genuine fans. How do I handle this?

expected.output_sample:
A toxic comment section left unmanaged signals to your genuine community that you don't value their safety. Here's the intervention framework.

**Immediate Actions (first 2 hours):**

**1. Platform moderation tools:**
- Instagram: Hidden Words feature — add specific slurs and toxic keywords to auto-hide. Any comment containing those words disappears immediately. Takes 5 minutes to set up.
- TikTok: Comment filtering (Settings → Privacy → Comments → Filter Keywords)
- YouTube: Hold potentially inappropriate comments for review (Studio → Community → Autofilter)

**2. Pin a comment from yourself** that sets the tone you want. Your pinned comment becomes the most visible one and anchors what kind of community this is. Example: 'This is a community for [what you're about]. Be kind to each other — I delete and block without warning.'

**3. Block vs. delete decision:**
- Delete: Removes the comment but the person can return. Use for first offenses.
- Block: Removes and prevents return. Use for repeated or severe harassment.

**4. Address it directly (once):** A brief statement — 'This community is for [people who connect with my music]. Harassment gets blocked immediately.' Don't apologize for having standards. Don't engage with the harassers.

**Running `analyze_sentiment`** to identify the comment patterns and worst offenders, and `dispatch_community_webhook` to activate automated moderation rules.

**Longer term:**
Clear, publicly posted community guidelines give you defensible grounds for moderation decisions. 'I deleted your comment per my community guidelines' is more defensible than 'I deleted your comment because I wanted to.'

scenario: Expert: Recovering reach after an Instagram algorithm drop

input.user_message: My Instagram reach dropped by about 60% over the last 3 weeks. I haven't changed anything about my posting — same type of content, same frequency. What's happening and how do I recover?

expected.output_sample:
A 60% reach drop with no behavior change typically indicates one of three things: an algorithm update, a shadow classification change, or engagement rate decay.

**Diagnosis First:**
Running `analyze_trends` to compare your engagement patterns before/after the drop. The key metrics:
- **Save rate change?** Instagram's algorithm weights saves heavily — if your save rate dropped, reach drops follow
- **Share rate change?** Shares are the highest-value engagement signal
- **Profile visit rate?** Low profile visits suggest you're not being surfaced to new users

**Common causes of sudden 60% drops:**

**1. Algorithm update:** Instagram periodically reweights different content types. Reels vs. carousel vs. static image weighting changes every 3–4 months. If you were optimized for one format and it's now deweighted, reach drops.

**2. Engagement rate decay:** If your recent posts received lower engagement (fewer saves/shares/comments), Instagram's system classifies your content as lower-quality — distribution reduces to protect user experience.

**3. Shadowban-adjacent classification:** If any recent posts used flagged hashtags, included deceptive elements, or were reported — Instagram's system reduces distribution without notification.

**Recovery Protocol:**
1. **Post a high-value saves-targeted piece** — carousels with genuinely useful content drive saves disproportionately. Saves signal quality to the algorithm.
2. **Go dormant on quantity, increase quality**: 3 posts/week with high save rate > 7 posts/week with low engagement
3. **Drive Reels traffic to your main feed**: Instagram currently prioritizes accounts that succeed in Reels to the rest of the algorithm
4. **Engage in your niche for 20 minutes before posting** — comment genuinely on other creators' content, then post your own. The activity signals to the algorithm that you're an active, engaged user.

**Recovery timeline:** 2–4 weeks of consistent high-engagement content typically restores reach.
expected.notes: Expert: Instagram reach drop recovery — diagnosis, save-rate focus, Reels bridge, engagement timing

**Tweet 4 (The Reveal Visual):** Just the album artwork. No text. Let it breathe.

**Tweet 5 (The Details):** Title, release date, pre-save link. The practical information.

**Tweet 6 (The Sample):** Audio/video clip. 30 seconds maximum. The music does the rest of the selling.

- Do NOT schedule the full thread at once — the gaps between tweets create anticipation as the thread builds

scenario: Expert: Cross-platform content strategy with platform-native adaptation

input.user_message: I'm active on Instagram, TikTok, YouTube, and X. I don't have time to create completely unique content for each platform. What's the most efficient approach that doesn't look lazy?

expected.output_sample:
Efficient cross-platform content is about ADAPTATION, not duplication. The same raw material should look native on each platform — not copy-pasted.

**The Content Pillar → Derivative Framework:**

**Create ONE high-quality 'pillar' piece per week:**
This is your most effort-intensive content. Could be a YouTube video, a detailed BTS session, or a significant moment in your creative process.

**Derive from it for each platform:**

**YouTube (the pillar itself):**
3–8 minute video. More context, more depth. Evergreen discovery potential via search.

**TikTok (the hook):**
Extract the most compelling 30–60 second moment from your YouTube video. Native TikTok requires:
- The hook in the FIRST 1.5 seconds (this is non-negotiable for FYP placement)
- Vertical format (re-record or crop)
- On-screen text captions (TikTok audiences are 40% sound-off)
- Post natively (don't use TikTok's built-in sharing from Instagram — TikTok's algorithm deprioritizes content with Instagram watermarks)

**Instagram (the context):**
The moment + the story around it. Carousel works well here — swipe-through context that TikTok doesn't support. Caption can be longer.

**X/Twitter (the thought):**
The one sentence that captures the insight from the pillar. Not a summary — a standalone thought that provokes engagement even without the video.

**What to avoid:**
Posting a TikTok directly to Instagram Reels as a cross-post (the TikTok watermark reduces Instagram distribution). Create format-specific versions.

scenario: Expert: Leveraging comment strategy to boost algorithmic reach

input.user_message: My posts get decent engagement from followers but I'm struggling to reach new people. I've heard comment section strategy matters. How do I use comments to expand reach?

expected.output_sample:
Comment strategy is one of the highest-leverage, zero-cost tactics for algorithmic reach expansion.

**Why Comments Drive Discovery:**
All major platforms (Instagram, TikTok, YouTube) treat comments as strong positive engagement signals — especially comments that generate REPLIES and extended conversation threads. A post with 20 comments and 10 reply chains outperforms a post with 100 likes and no comments in algorithmic distribution.

**Tactics That Work:**

**1. End posts with a specific question:**
Not 'what do you think?' (too generic) — a question with a binary or specific answer that's easy to respond to:
'Which side are you on: [Option A] or [Option B]?'
'What song do you always skip to in your library?'
The easier the response, the higher the comment count.

**2. Reply to every comment in the first 60 minutes:**
Comments answered create reply chains. Reply chains increase comment count. Higher comment counts signal popularity to the algorithm. The first 60-90 minutes after posting is when algorithmic scoring is heaviest — be available.

**3. Comment on larger accounts in your niche:**
Not generic comments ('Love this!'). Specific, substantive observations that other fans would want to respond to. When your comment is good enough to get replies from a large account's audience, those interactions drive profile visits to yours.

**4. 'Controversy without drama':**
The most viral posts are mildly provocative — strong opinions that people feel compelled to agree OR disagree with. Not drama — a professional opinion about something in your field that reasonable people could debate.

Running `analyze_trends` to identify which of your recent posts drove the most comment thread depth — that tells you which content generates the conversation the algorithm rewards.
expected.notes: Expert: comment strategy — reply chains, specific questions, first-60-minute window, comment on larger accounts

scenario: Expert: Converting casual followers into active fan advocates

input.user_message: I have 40K Instagram followers but they feel passive — they consume but don't share, don't comment, don't seem emotionally invested. How do I turn passive followers into active fans?

Passive followers are consuming your content but haven't made an IDENTITY investment in your work. The transition from passive to active requires creating belonging — a reason to participate, not just observe.

**The Fan Activation Pathway:**

**1. Create participation moments:**
Content that invites a specific action (not just 'like if you agree'). Examples:
- 'Caption this' with a photo
- 'I need your help naming this track — Option A or Option B'
- 'Send me a voice message with what this song means to you' (Instagram DMs)
Participation creates micro-investment. Once someone does something for you, they're psychologically more attached.

**2. Acknowledge them back:**
Public acknowledgment converts fans to advocates instantly. Screen-capture fan comments and post them (with permission). Name-check fans in stories. When your audience feels seen, they become evangelists.

**3. Create an 'insider' identity:**
Your most engaged fans want a name, a community identity, a signal that they're 'the real ones.' This can be as simple as a nickname for your fanbase that only true followers know ('If you know, you know' dynamics).

**4. Ask directly:**
The most underused tactic: just ask your fans to share. Not as a marketing appeal — as a personal request:
'If this song means something to you, share it with one person who needs to hear it. That's how independent music grows.'

**5. Super fan identification:**
Running `analyze_sentiment` to identify your most consistently engaged followers — the top 1–2% who respond to almost everything. These are your core community. A DM to 10 super fans asking them to share a new post drives more impact than posting to 40,000.

scenario: Expert: Instagram grid aesthetic and profile strategy

input.user_message: I want my Instagram grid to look intentional and cohesive — not random. But I don't want it to look so planned that it looks fake. How do I build a visual grid strategy that's authentic?

**What Creates Grid Cohesion:**
The most cohesive grids don't have identical-looking images — they have consistent:
- **Color palette** (warm vs. cool tones, high vs. low saturation)
- **Editing treatment** (same filter/preset applied to all photos creates visual unity)
- **Subject variety within consistent mood** (portraits, environments, objects — all with the same quality of light and color treatment)

**What Kills Grid Cohesion:**
- Mixing phone snapshots with professional photos without consistent editing
- Different color temperatures across photos (some warm, some cool, no pattern)
- Screenshot reposts that break the visual flow
- Text-heavy promotional graphics with no aesthetic treatment

**Practical Implementation:**

**Step 1: Choose a grading preset**
Lightroom presets or VSCO filters apply consistently across photos. Choose ONE preset that matches your brand's color language and apply it to all posts. This single decision creates 80% of your grid cohesion.

**Step 2: The content variety rule**
Alternate post types to prevent visual monotony:
- Portrait/close-up → environment → object → portrait
This creates visual rhythm without requiring identical content.

**Step 3: 3-photo row planning**
Instagram grids display in rows of 3. Preview your row composition before posting — each row of 3 should have visual balance (not 3 identical dark close-up photos in a row).

scenario: Expert: Live streaming strategy for artist audience building

input.user_message: Should I be doing live streams? I've tried a few but they felt awkward and I got very few viewers. I don't know if it's worth the discomfort.

expected.output_sample:
Whether live streaming is worth it depends entirely on your audience and what type of content you're creating — it's not universally valuable.

**When Live Streaming Is Worth It:**
- Q&A sessions where the live format creates authentic real-time connection
- Milestone moments (release day listen-throughs, performance milestones)
- Behind-the-scenes production sessions (fans watching you make music live)
- Interactive fan sessions before a major release

**Why Your Lives Are Feeling Awkward:**
Akward lives almost always have the same cause: starting without an agenda and waiting for viewers to show up. You're live for 3 minutes with 0 viewers — that's inherently uncomfortable. The fix:
1. Announce the live 24 hours in advance with a specific topic: 'Live Friday 7pm ET — first listen-through of the new album with me and Q&A'
2. Start immediately with content (don't wait for viewers) — those watching the replay see a person already engaged, not awkwardly waiting
3. End with a specific call-to-action: 'Replay goes into my Stories after this'

**Viewer Count Reality:**
For most independent artists under 50K followers, expecting more than 50–100 simultaneous live viewers is unrealistic. The VALUE is the replay — Instagram/TikTok live replays reach 5–10x the live viewer count. Optimize for the replay.

**The Frequency Question:**
Consistent low-friction lives (20–30 minutes, specific topic, weekly or bi-weekly) compound over time. One awkward live proves nothing. 10 consistent lives builds an audience.

Running `analyze_trends` to identify which content format drives your highest current engagement — that tells you whether live fits your content ecosystem.
expected.notes: Expert: live streaming strategy — announcement, replay optimization, viewer count reality, when it's worth it

scenario: Expert: Optimal posting frequency and content calendar science

input.user_message: I've been told I need to post every day. But when I post every day my quality drops and I burn out. How do I find the right posting frequency for sustainable growth?

expected.output_sample:
The 'post every day' advice is cargo-cult social media wisdom that ignores the quality-frequency tradeoff.

**The Algorithm Reality:**
Every platform weights ENGAGEMENT RATE over raw post frequency. A post that gets 5% engagement is distributed much more broadly than a post that gets 1% engagement — even if you're posting 7x as often. Posting low-quality content frequently actually TRAINS the algorithm that your content is low-engagement, reducing your overall distribution.

**Platform-Specific Sustainable Frequencies:**

**TikTok**: 1–3x per week (not daily). One high-quality video that hooks in the first second outperforms 7 rushed videos. The algorithm has very long memory — posts can go viral weeks after posting.

**Instagram**:
- Feed posts: 3–4x/week (quality threshold matters most)
- Stories: Daily is fine — Stories are consumption-focused, not distribution-competitive
- Reels: 2–3x/week

**YouTube**: 1x/week or bi-weekly. Long-form YouTube rewards consistency over frequency. One video every 2 weeks with strong SEO beats daily shorts that don't compound.

**X/Twitter**: 1–3x/day works if the content is genuinely worth the post. Thoughtful takes on your industry or craft outperform volume.

**The Batching Solution:**
Create content in sessions, not in real-time. Dedicate 2–3 hours once per week to produce and schedule 4–5 posts. `create_social_calendar` with batch scheduling eliminates daily production pressure.

Running `analyze_trends` on your historical post performance — engagement rate by posting frequency — to find YOUR optimal cadence (not a generic recommendation).
expected.notes: Expert: posting frequency — engagement rate > volume, platform-specific cadences, batching solution

**Legal Track (immediate — today):**

scenario: TikTok algorithm recovery after follower purge
framing: An artist's TikTok account lost 40% of its followers overnight due to a platform bot purge. Their organic reach has dropped significantly. They need a recovery strategy that rebuilds genuine algorithmic momentum without triggering further penalties.

input.user_message: TikTok wiped out 40% of my followers overnight — apparently a bot purge. My views dropped from 50k average to under 5k. I didn't buy any bots — I think I was collateral damage. How do I recover my algorithm standing? Do I need to post more or less? Should I delete old videos? And is there anything I can do with TikTok support to appeal?

expected.output_sample:
Algorithm recovery after a bot purge is possible but takes 2-4 weeks of consistent action. Here's the recovery strategy:

**Understanding What Happened:**
TikTok's bot purges affect legitimate accounts whose content was shared by bot networks (without your knowledge). Your follower count dropped, but more importantly, your account's 'trust score' in the algorithm may have flagged as suspicious. The algorithm temporarily restricts distribution while it re-evaluates your engagement patterns.

**Recovery Actions:**

**Week 1-2: Re-establish Authentic Engagement Signals**
1. Post 1x/day (not more — oversaturation confuses the algorithm during a flagged period)
2. Focus on your BEST performing video format — whatever previously got 50k+ views. Don't experiment during recovery.
3. Target completion rate: aim for content that people watch completely. 8-15 second hooks that deliver on their promise perform best for completion.
4. Engage actively in comments on your own content within the first 30 minutes of posting — early engagement signals authentic activity
5. DO NOT delete old videos — TikTok's algorithm uses your historical performance data to calibrate distribution. Deleting content resets that calibration.

**Week 3-4: Growth Signals**
1. Add one strong Call to Action in your videos: 'Follow for [specific value]' — rebuilding genuine follower conversions
2. Duet/Stitch high-performing content in your niche — this routes you through an active content graph
3. Respond to comments with video responses — this generates additional algorithmic events from one piece of content

**TikTok Appeal:**
Use TikTok's in-app feedback/report mechanism: Settings → Support → Report a problem. Select 'Account issue' and report that you believe your account was incorrectly impacted by a spam/bot removal action. TikTok rarely manually intervenes but you should document the appeal. Include: your account handle, approximate date of follower drop, and statement that you have never purchased followers.

**Realistic Timeline:**
2-4 weeks to return to 20-30k average views. Full recovery to 50k+ typically takes 4-8 weeks of consistent authentic content.

adversarial: What if the account continues to lose followers even after implementing the recovery strategy?
notes: TikTok algorithm recovery after bot purge is a real and painful scenario for artists — covers the recovery sequence and realistic expectations

scenario: Instagram Reels vs. TikTok cross-posting strategy and platform cannibalization
framing: An artist is posting the same content on TikTok and Instagram Reels simultaneously and their reach has plateaued on both. They need to understand whether cross-posting identical content hurts algorithmic performance, how to adapt content per platform, and whether there's a priority order.

input.user_message: I've been posting the exact same videos on TikTok and Instagram Reels at the same time. My reach has plateaued — is cross-posting killing my performance? Do these platforms penalize you for watermarked TikTok videos? And if I have to make separate content, how different does it actually need to be?

expected.output_sample:
Cross-posting is causing at least part of your plateau. Here's what's actually happening and the fix:

**The Watermark Problem:**
Instagram explicitly deprioritizes videos with TikTok watermarks in Reels distribution. This was confirmed by Instagram's head of product in 2021 and has remained policy. The TikTok watermark signals 'cross-posted content' to Instagram's algorithm, which reduces distribution to Explore and Reels feeds. This is a confirmed penalty, not speculation.

**The Content Recognition Problem:**
Both platforms use visual fingerprinting. When the same video appears on both platforms simultaneously, each algorithm may recognize it as already-circulating content and reduce novel distribution. The algorithm rewards fresh content that's in its own discovery cycle.

**The Audience Behavior Problem:**
Your best fans may follow you on BOTH platforms. When they see the same content twice in the same week, they're less likely to engage with the second instance — reducing your engagement rate per view on the second platform, which signals quality drop to the algorithm.

**How Different Does It Actually Need to Be?**
Not a complete re-shoot — but specifically:
1. **Remove the TikTok watermark**: Export TikTok videos without watermark (TikTok has a native 'save without watermark' option; or use CapCut which doesn't add watermarks natively)
2. **Offset posting timing**: Post TikTok Monday, Instagram Reels Wednesday — 48-hour gap breaks the simultaneous signal
3. **Change the hook or opening 3 seconds**: The algorithm's fingerprinting is strongest on the first few frames. Trim or re-shoot just the opening.
4. **Different caption/text overlay**: Platform-native text styles (TikTok text vs. Reels text) also signal native creation

**Priority Order:**
Post on whichever platform has stronger organic performance for you first. Let it have a 24-48 hour runway before cross-posting. Usually: TikTok for discovery (larger organic reach potential), Instagram for conversion (followers → streams, followers → email list).

adversarial: What if the artist's audience is primarily on one platform and the other is very small?
notes: Cross-platform posting strategy is a core social media question — covers the TikTok watermark penalty, content fingerprinting, and timing offset strategy

scenario: Community management crisis — toxic fan behavior targeting another artist
framing: An artist's fanbase is harassing another artist online following a perceived slight. The behavior is going viral and the artist is being held responsible for their fanbase's actions. The social media manager needs to address this without alienating the fan community.

input.user_message: Our fanbase is harassing Artist B online because they think Artist B disrespected our artist in an interview. The harassment is trending on Twitter and our artist is being called out for not stopping it. We can't control 50k fans but we're getting the blame. How do we address this publicly? What do we post? And how do we de-escalate without making the fans feel attacked?

expected.output_sample:
Fan harassment incidents require a statement that's firm without being punitive. Here's the playbook:

**The Core Dilemma:**
You can't control fans, but the artist CAN control whether their platform endorses the behavior. Silence = implicit endorsement in public perception. The statement must come from the artist directly, not management.

**The Statement Structure (Post on artist's main account within 24 hours):**

DO include:
- Clear, unambiguous statement that harassment is not acceptable, not in the artist's name, not what the artist wants from their community
- Specific language: 'harassment,' 'bullying,' 'online attacks' — not vague 'negativity'
- A positive redirect: 'My fans are better than this' + 'channel your energy toward [positive thing]'

DON'T include:
- Relitigating what Artist B said — that feeds the conflict
- Victimizing the artist over the fans' behavior
- Threatening consequences (fans will perceive this as an attack)

**Sample Language:**
'To anyone sending hate to [Artist B] in my name — stop. That's not what I stand for and it's not how I want my community to operate. I can speak for myself. If you want to support me, support my music. Harassment toward anyone is never okay, full stop.'

**De-escalation in Fan Channels:**
Post the same message in any official Discord, Telegram, or fan group you manage. Pin it. Moderators in those spaces should enforce no-harassment rules for the following 48-72 hours.

**What NOT to Do:**
- Do NOT delete comments on your posts calling out the behavior — that looks like suppression
- Do NOT like or share any posts from fans that frame the harassment as defending the artist
- Do NOT engage with Artist B directly — that creates a headline

**Timeline:**
Post within 4-6 hours of becoming aware if the story is actively trending. After 12+ hours without a statement, the narrative has moved to 'artist enables harassment.'

adversarial: What if a significant portion of the fanbase turns against the artist for posting the de-escalation statement?
notes: Fan community harassment crisis is a real social media management scenario — covers statement structure, de-escalation tactics, and what not to do

scenario: YouTube channel optimization — algorithm, metadata, and community tab strategy
framing: An artist has 85k YouTube subscribers but their videos average only 8k views — far below what their subscriber count should deliver. Their CTR (click-through rate) is 3.2% and average view duration is 38%. They need a diagnostic and optimization plan.

input.user_message: I have 85k YouTube subscribers but my videos only get 8k views on average. My CTR is 3.2% and average view duration is 38%. YouTube Studio shows my audience is mostly inactive. What do I do to fix this? Is my low CTR a thumbnail problem? And what are the best practices for music video metadata — titles, descriptions, tags?

expected.output_sample:
Your metrics tell a clear story — here's the diagnosis and fix:

**Diagnosis:**
- **CTR 3.2%**: Below YouTube's average CTR of 4-10% for established channels. This is a thumbnail + title problem — your content is being shown to people but not compelling them to click.
- **38% average view duration**: On a 4-minute video, that's 91 seconds. For music videos specifically, 40-50% is closer to average. You're losing viewers early — first 30 seconds need work.
- **85k subscribers, 8k views = 9.4% view rate**: A healthy channel should see 10-20% of subscribers view new content. You're at the bottom of the range, indicating subscriber staleness (old subscribers who no longer engage).

**Fix 1: Thumbnail Optimization (Biggest Impact)**
Your 3.2% CTR is driven by thumbnails. Best practices for music content:
- High-contrast face shot — faces with emotional expression drive CTR 20-30% higher than graphic-only thumbnails
- Bold 2-3 word text overlay (if any) — high contrast, readable at mobile size
- Test at 100x100 pixels — if it's not readable/identifiable at that size, it fails on mobile
- A/B test: Replace your current thumbnail with a new design and check CTR after 500 impressions

**Fix 2: Title Optimization**

scenario: Social media crisis from an old resurfaced post
framing: A 7-year-old controversial post from an artist's personal account has been screenshotted and is going viral. The artist was 19 at the time and the views expressed were immature. The social media team needs to advise on a response that's authentic without being dismissive.

input.user_message: Someone dug up a 7-year-old tweet from when our artist was 19. It says something racially insensitive that doesn't represent who they are today. The screenshot is going viral right now. Do we delete the original tweet? Do we respond, and if so, with what? How do we handle this in the next 6 hours?

expected.output_sample:
This is a high-stakes crisis requiring decisive action in the next 2 hours, not 6. Here's the protocol:

**Step 1: Delete the Original Post (Immediately)**
Delete the original tweet now. Yes, screenshots exist — the post will circulate regardless. But leaving it up signals indifference. Deletion is a necessary first act, not sufficient on its own. Deletion without a statement looks like suppression; deletion WITH a statement is accountability.

**Step 2: Draft the Statement (Within 2 Hours)**
The statement must come from the artist in their own voice — not from management. The anatomy of an effective apology for this scenario:

1. **Acknowledge specifically** (not 'if anyone was offended') — describe the specific nature of the post without requoting it
2. **Ownership** — 'I said this. It was wrong.'
3. **Context without excuse** — 'I was 19 and said something I now recognize as harmful. Age doesn't excuse it — it explains the ignorance, not the impact.'
4. **Growth statement** — What's changed? What does the artist understand now that they didn't then?
5. **Action** — What are they doing beyond the apology? (Donate to a relevant organization, commit to listening, etc.)

**Sample Framework:**
'Seven years ago I posted something racially insensitive. I've deleted it, but I want to be direct: it was wrong. I was young and ignorant in ways I'm not proud of. That post doesn't represent who I am today, but I understand that 'who I am today' is something I have to show, not just say. I'm [specific action]. I'm sorry to anyone that post caused harm.'

**What NOT to Do:**
- Do NOT post 'I was hacked'
- Do NOT claim the context was different (it wasn't)
- Do NOT over-explain or make it about other positive things you've done
- Do NOT wait for this to blow over — it won't

**Platform Sequence:**
Post on Twitter first (where the story is), then Instagram caption simultaneously, then any other active platforms.

adversarial: What if the artist refuses to apologize because they believe the post was taken out of context?
notes: Resurfaced old post crisis is one of the most common social media crises for artists — covers the delete decision, statement structure, and what not to do

scenario: Analytics interpretation and ROI measurement for social media investment

How do I connect the dots between Instagram metrics and actual revenue? And what should I be tracking that I'm probably not tracking yet?

The gap between social metrics and business outcomes is the key challenge. Here's how to close it:

**The Metrics That Actually Matter (Correlate with Revenue):**

1. **Profile Visits → Streaming Link Clicks**: In Instagram Insights → Profile Activity, you can see how many people visited your profile after seeing a post. Cross-reference with your Linktree/SmartURL click data for the same period. A 10% conversion from profile visit to streaming link click is a meaningful attribution chain.

2. **Spotify for Artists → Audience Source**: Spotify shows where new listeners are coming from. 'Social media' is one of the source categories. If your Instagram campaigns correlate with spikes in Spotify's social media source data — that's direct ROI.

3. **TikTok Sound Analytics**: When your song audio is used in TikTok videos, TikTok shows you how many people clicked through to streaming from that sound page. This is one of the clearest direct-attribution metrics available.

4. **UTM Links for Tour Ticket Attribution**: If you're posting about shows, use UTM-tagged links in your bio/stories: `ticketmaster.com/artist?utm_source=instagram&utm_medium=stories`. Your ticketing platform analytics will show sales attributed to those links.

**Create a Correlation Report:**
For the past 3 months, plot:
- Weekly Instagram story views + TikTok views (y-axis: social reach)
- Weekly Spotify monthly listener count (y-axis: streaming)

2. **Story reply rate** — replies are the highest-engagement signal, correlating with superfan conversion

4. **Benchmark: engagement rate against audience size** — a 4% engagement rate with 50k followers is excellent; the same rate with 5k followers is below average

**The Manager-Friendly Report:**
'In Q3, social content drove [X] Spotify source clicks, [Y] ticket link clicks, and [Z] new followers. Compared to Q2, streaming monthly listeners grew [X%] which correlates with [specific campaign]. Here's the 3-month chart.'

Creator seeding for music on TikTok is one of the most effective promotional tactics available. Here's the full framework:

**Paid vs. Organic:**
- **Organic seeding**: You reach out to creators and ask them to use your song for free. Success rate: 5-15% for cold outreach. Works best if your song has a clear creative prompt (dance, comedy, emotional, challenge).
- **Paid seeding**: You pay creators to post videos using your song. Typical rates: Micro (50k-500k followers) = $100-$500 per video; Mid-tier (500k-2M) = $500-$2,500; Macro (2M+) = $2,500+.
- **Hybrid**: Pay micro-creators as the seed wave; hope organic creators pick it up once there's momentum. This is the most cost-effective approach.

**How to Identify the Right Creators:**
1. Go to TikTok and search for your genre's hashtags + related emotional tags (#rnb #sad_music #chill_vibes etc.)
2. Look for creators who already use music similar to yours — their audience already resonates with the sound
3. Check: engagement rate (comments + likes ÷ views) > 3% is good for TikTok
4. Check: does the creator make music-forward content (dancing, lip sync, POV scenarios with music)? Or is music incidental to their content?
5. Tools: Creator Marketplace in TikTok Business, Modash, or Sprout Social for creator discovery

**Deal Structure:**
For paid deals:
- Creator uses your song (specifically, the TikTok sound for your track) in 1 video
- Video goes live on a specific date (coordinate with your release week for concentration)
- No requirement to say they were paid (FTC requires a disclosure hashtag #ad or #sponsored — make sure creators include this)
- Deliverable: 1 posted video, stats shared after 72 hours
- Do NOT dictate the creative concept — let the creator use their natural format with your song. Forced creative underperforms.

**Measuring Results:**
Primary metric: TikTok Sound Page views and videos created under your sound. After creator posts:
1. Check your TikTok Sound Page (accessible from your distributor's TikTok analytics or directly in-app): how many videos now use your sound? How many views total?
2. Spotify spike: Check Spotify for Artists for a spike in daily listeners in the 48 hours after the creator wave posts
3. Save rate: New playlist saves on Spotify during the campaign correlate with creator-driven discovery

adversarial: What if a paid creator posts a video with the song but it gets terrible engagement and hurts the campaign?

scenario: TikTok algorithm recovery — rebuilding reach after account suppression

input.user_message: My TikTok account had 45K followers and was getting 100K+ views per video. Two months ago, something happened and now my videos are stuck at 200-500 views despite posting every day. I haven't posted anything that would violate terms. What caused this and how do I recover?
input.context: TikTok creator, 45K followers, sudden view drop from 100K+ to 200-500 per video, 2 months duration

expected.output_sample:
TikTok Account Suppression Recovery:

**Diagnosing the Suppression:**
Your symptoms are consistent with what creators call a 'shadowban' — a throttling of distribution that limits your videos to follower feeds only (or even a subset). With 45K followers getting 200-500 views, you're at <1% follower view rate — the algorithm has essentially stopped distributing your content beyond your immediate audience.

**Common Causes (Even Without Clear Violation):**
1. **Engagement rate collapse**: If your followers stopped engaging with you (saves, shares, comments), TikTok's algorithm deprioritizes your content. This creates a death spiral: low distribution → fewer people to engage → lower engagement rate → even lower distribution.
2. **Posting frequency shift**: Sudden changes in posting cadence (either direction) can disrupt algorithm patterns.
3. **Content pivot**: If you changed content type, the algorithm needs to re-learn your audience — initial distribution suffers.
4. **Mass follower acquisition spike**: If you ran a giveaway or viral moment that brought followers who aren't genuinely interested, your engagement rate dropped, signaling low-quality audience to the algorithm.
5. **External link behavior**: Heavy use of link-in-bio CTAs that take users off-platform can trigger reduced distribution.

**Recovery Strategy (4-6 weeks):**

**Phase 1: Content Reset (Week 1–2)**
Stop posting for 3–5 days. This sounds counterintuitive but allows any active throttle to expire. Then:
- Create 5 videos in your best-performing genre/style from 6+ months ago — go back to what worked
- Post at 3x/week (not daily — too much frequency with low engagement makes numbers worse)
- First video of the restart: make it your best possible content. Its performance sets the new baseline.

**Phase 2: Engagement Stimulation (Weeks 1–4)**
- Respond to every comment within 1 hour of posting (active creator signal)
- Use TikTok LIVE for 15–20 minutes weekly — live engagement is heavily weighted
- Duet or stitch with accounts in your niche — their audience sees you, expanding reach
- Post at your historically highest-performing time (check TikTok Analytics → Followers → Most Active)

**Phase 3: Monitor Metrics**
Track weekly: average views per video, follower-to-view ratio, share rate. If improving → maintain strategy. If flat after 4 weeks → consider a fresh secondary account while keeping the primary.

**Nuclear Option:** Some creators with established audiences start a second account, build fresh momentum with similar content (no cross-promotion until it's growing), then reconnect audiences when the algorithm picks up the new account.

notes: R7 expert — TikTok algorithm suppression recovery, shadowban diagnosis, engagement reset strategy

scenario: Community moderation crisis — coordinated harassment campaign response

Coordinated Harassment Response — Immediate Protocol:

**In the Next 30 Minutes:**

1. **Document Everything First**: Screenshot the Discord coordination (if you can access it) and ALL harassing comments with timestamps. This evidence is critical for platform appeals and, if needed, legal action.

2. **Enable Restricted Comments on TikTok**: TikTok → Profile → Settings → Privacy → Comments → 'Friends Only' or 'No one.' This immediately stops the comment flood. You can re-open to 'Everyone' after the campaign subsides.

3. **Instagram Comment Filters**: Instagram → Settings → Privacy → Comments → 'Advanced Comment Filtering' ON + add specific slur/harassment keywords to the hidden words list. This auto-hides harassing comments without deleting them.

4. **Block Accounts in Bulk**: There are tools (e.g., TikTok's block by keyword for specific patterns) that help block accounts matching patterns. For Instagram, use third-party tools like Combin to block by comment text.

File reports on BOTH platforms for 'coordinated inauthentic behavior' and 'harassment campaign':
- TikTok: Settings → Report → 'Coordinated harassment'
- Instagram: Profile → Report → 'Harassment or bullying'
Include your screenshot evidence. Platforms take coordinated mass-reporting more seriously when counter-evidence is provided.

**If Your Account Gets Suspended:**
Don't panic — coordinated mass reports sometimes trigger auto-suspension even on innocent accounts. Both platforms have appeals processes:
- TikTok: Email/form appeal within 24 hours of suspension notice
- Instagram: Support → Something Went Wrong → 'My account was unfairly disabled'

**Don't Post During Active Campaign**: New posts during a harassment campaign give them fresh targets and may include content with lower-than-normal engagement (harassing accounts don't engage positively), which could signal the algorithm negatively.

**Mental Health Priority**: Coordinated harassment is distressing regardless of platform outcome. Tell someone you trust, step away from monitoring comments, and consider setting up a trusted friend to monitor notifications temporarily so you don't have to see everything in real time.

scenario: Social media analytics interpretation — identifying actionable signals vs vanity metrics

input.user_message: I have access to Instagram Insights, TikTok Analytics, and YouTube Studio. I have no idea which metrics actually matter vs. which are just ego metrics. Can you tell me which 5 metrics I should obsess over for growing a music artist account, and what actions I should take based on each metric?
input.context: Music artist with accounts on Instagram, TikTok, YouTube wanting to focus on actionable metrics

expected.output_sample:
5 Metrics That Actually Matter for Music Artist Growth:

**Metric 1: Save Rate (Instagram) / Stitch/Duet Rate (TikTok)**
What it measures: Did someone value this content enough to save it for later or riff on it?
Why it matters: Saves signal 'genuine interest' to both Instagram and TikTok algorithms, triggering broader distribution. Stitch/duet means your sound or content is being amplified by others.
Benchmark: 5%+ save rate on Instagram = strong content. Any stitch/duet on TikTok = content worth repeating.
Action if low: Your content isn't being perceived as valuable or reference-worthy. Ask: is there a practical 'save-worthy' element (list, tip, resource) or an emotional element (vulnerable, relatable, inspiring) that makes people want to return to it?

**Metric 2: Shares / Sends (All Platforms)**
What it measures: Did someone send this to a friend via DM?
Why it matters: Shares are the highest-intent engagement action. Platform algorithms heavily favor share-generating content because it signals real-world relevance.
Benchmark: 1–5% share rate is strong. Under 0.3% = content isn't culturally resonant enough to share.
Action if low: Ask yourself 'would someone send this to a specific person they know?' If you can't identify who or why, rethink the content angle.

**Metric 3: Completion Rate / Watch Time % (TikTok, YouTube, Reels)**
What it measures: What % of your video do people actually watch?
Why it matters: Completion rate is the primary signal for algorithmic distribution. A 100% completion rate on a 15-sec video distributes better than 30% completion on a 60-sec video.
Benchmark: TikTok: 70%+ completion is excellent. YouTube: 40%+ average view duration.
Action if low: Your hook isn't landing OR you're losing people at a specific moment. In TikTok Analytics → Video Data → Retention graph — identify where people drop off and fix that moment.

**Metric 4: Profile/Bio Link Clicks (Instagram → Linktree/Website)**
What it measures: How many viewers convert from content to action outside the platform?
Why it matters: For music artists, converting viewers to email subscribers, Spotify followers, or stream clicks is the goal. Profile visit → bio click = funnel conversion.
Benchmark: 1–3% of reach clicking through = healthy for music artist content.
Action if low: Your CTA isn't specific enough. 'Link in bio' is weaker than 'Spotify link in bio for new track.' Update your CTA on every post.

**Metric 5: Follower Growth Rate (Not Follower Count)**
What it measures: Month-over-month % growth, not raw number.
Why it matters: Raw follower count is a lagging vanity metric. Growth rate tells you whether your content strategy is working right now.
Benchmark: 3–7% monthly growth rate = strong for organic content. Under 1% = content isn't attracting new followers.
Action if low: You're good at retaining but not converting new viewers to followers. Add a direct follow ask in your video ('Follow so you don't miss the next one') — simple CTAs move the number.

scenario: YouTube channel SEO and algorithmic growth strategy

input.user_message: My YouTube channel has 8,000 subscribers but my videos average only 400 views. My click-through rate from impressions is 2.1% and average view duration is 38% on 6-minute videos. Break down exactly what's wrong and give me a concrete optimization plan.
input.context: Artist channel, posts weekly music content, has 47 videos over 18 months

response_contains: CTR of 2.1% is below the 4-7% benchmark
38% retention means viewers drop off around 2:17
expected.output_sample:
Your numbers tell a clear story: two separate problems — discoverability (low CTR) and content structure (low retention). Here's the breakdown:

**Problem 1: CTR 2.1% (benchmark: 4–7%)**
YouTube serves your thumbnail+title combo ~19,000 times per video but only 400 people click. Fix:
- A/B test thumbnails using YouTube Studio's built-in split test (need 1,000+ impressions per variant)
- Faces with emotion outperform text-heavy thumbnails for music channels
- Title formula: [Emotion/Adjective] + [Song/Video Type] + [Artist Name] | avoid generic 'Official Video'
- First 48 hours velocity matters — pin comment, share to email list, Stories simultaneously at publish

**Problem 2: 38% retention on 6-min videos**
38% = viewers leave at ~2:17. YouTube's algorithm reads this as 'low satisfaction' and deprioritizes in feed.
- First 30 seconds: lead with the hook (best moment, conflict, unexpected visual) — no intro, no logo animation
- Pattern interrupt every 90 seconds (cut, B-roll, graphic, camera angle change)
- For music: 'making of' structure outperforms static performance — show process, not just result
- Target 50%+ retention; 60%+ is tier-1 signal

**Structural fixes:**
- Group videos into playlists by series — YouTube recommends within playlists aggressively
- End screen at 5:30 → link to playlist (not 'subscribe' button — subscribe is algorithmic anyway)
- Chapter markers reduce drop-off at inflection points

**30-day action:** Re-upload your top 5 performing videos with new thumbnails + optimized titles. Existing videos can be updated without losing watch history.

notes: R7 expert — YouTube CTR + retention dual diagnosis, concrete benchmarks, actionable remediation

scenario: Social listening strategy for competitive intelligence

input.user_message: How do I use social listening to understand what my audience actually wants and what's working for artists in my genre? I want a systematic approach, not just checking my mentions.
input.context: Independent R&B artist, wants to use data to inform content and release strategy

expected.output_sample:
Social listening at a strategic level has four distinct use cases. Here's the systematic approach:

**1. Audience language mining**
Search your artist name + genre terms across TikTok, Twitter, Reddit (r/rnb, r/hiphopheads). Look for: exact phrases fans use to describe your music, emotional language ('this song hits when…'), use-case context ('perfect for late night drives'). These phrases become your hook language for captions, bios, and ad copy.

**2. Competitor gap analysis**
Track 5 peers/comparables: [genre peer 1], [genre peer 2], etc. For each, monitor:
- Comment sentiment on their most recent releases (positive/negative ratio)
- Complaint patterns ('I wish they'd do more…', 'not as good as their old stuff')
- What content performs vs. what they post most
Gap = what their audience wants that they're not providing → your content opportunity

**3. Trend spike detection**
Monitor:
- TikTok trending sounds in your genre (TikTok Creative Center, free)
- Twitter/X trending topics in music news
- Reddit thread velocity (posts gaining comments fast in your subreddits)
Spike → 48-hour window to insert your content while algorithm rewards early movers

**4. Sentiment monitoring post-release**
First 72 hours after release: track sentiment ratio (positive:neutral:negative). Benchmark: >70% positive = healthy. Drop below 60% = investigate the cause (production feedback, artwork, title confusion). Sentiment data informs whether to push advertising spend or hold.

**Tools at this scale (free tier sufficient):**
- TikTok Creative Center, Spotify for Artists, YouTube Analytics
- Google Alerts for your name + genre keywords
- Reddit: sort by 'new' + keyword search in relevant subreddits

**Output:** Weekly 15-minute social listening audit → content calendar adjustments → release strategy inputs.

notes: R7 expert — structured social listening framework, competitive gap analysis, trend timing

scenario: Live streaming strategy for music audience growth

input.user_message: I want to start live streaming on Twitch and YouTube simultaneously to grow my audience as a producer. What's the setup, format, and growth strategy that actually works?
input.context: Beat producer with 5,000 followers across platforms, no live streaming experience

Live streaming for music producers is a proven growth channel when structured correctly. Here's the full setup:

**Simulcast setup:**
Use Restream.io (free tier) or OBS with multiple RTMP outputs to stream to Twitch and YouTube simultaneously. Audio routing: DAW → virtual audio cable (Loopback on Mac, VoiceMeeter on PC) → OBS. This prevents latency issues from monitoring your own output.

**Format that works:**
- 'Beat from scratch in [time]' — specific challenge constraint drives watch completion
- Sample flip sessions — explain every decision, builds educational value
- 'Rate my beat' — interactive format, chat drives the session
- Collab sessions with other producers (cross-promotion, dual audience access)
- Aim for 90–120 min sessions; sub-90 minutes doesn't reward algorithmically on Twitch

**Stream title SEO (YouTube and Twitch search both index this):**
- Include genre + style: 'Making a Boom Bap Beat LIVE | Dark Sample Flip'
- NOT: 'vibe session 🎹' — zero search volume

**Consistency beats quality:**
- Same day, same time, every week — 'Every Tuesday 8pm ET'
- Twitch rewards consistent streamers with Browse page placement after 12 consecutive weeks
- YouTube saves VODs — these become searchable content permanently

**The clip pipeline:**
- Every stream generates 3–5 clip-worthy moments (a fire beat drop, a mistake that turned into something, a chat interaction)
- Download stream VOD → clip in Premiere/CapCut → post to TikTok/Reels/YouTube Shorts within 24 hours
- One 2-hour stream = 5 short-form posts = your entire week's content

**Growth levers:**
- Raid smaller producers after your stream ends (reciprocal audience exchange)
- Twitch /raid command + Discord 'going live' ping = baseline viewership on day 1
- 50 average concurrent viewers on Twitch = Twitch Affiliate eligibility
